title: Acquire vs Intercom for Power users
meta_description: If you expect the setup to grow long-term, Intercom fails first because support is primarily messaging-based without built-in video. See why Acquire holds up.
constraint_lens: You need a support tool that supports advanced, real-time interaction methods like video and co-browsing without hitting capability limits.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for power users who need room to grow.
one_second_verdict.reason: Intercom fails first because it breaks when support is primarily messaging-based without built-in video and co-browsing capabilities.

verdict.summary: Acquire is the better choice when your support process depends on live interaction rather than messaging. It includes built-in video chat and co-browsing, allowing agents to directly guide customers in real time. Intercom is centered around messaging workflows, which work well for many cases but become limiting when deeper, real-time interaction is required.
verdict.decision_rule: If support is primarily messaging-based without built-in video and co-browsing capabilities, Intercom fails first.

sections.heading: Why Acquire fits this situation
sections.content: This setup fits a power user who needs to go beyond text-based support and interact with customers directly. Messaging introduces delays and misunderstandings when issues are complex. Acquire removes those limits by enabling real-time visual and verbal communication.

bullets.point: Built-in video chat allows agents to communicate with customers face-to-face during support sessions.
bullets.why_it_matters: This enables faster resolution of complex issues that are difficult to explain through text.

bullets.point: Co-browsing lets agents see and guide the customer’s screen in real time.
bullets.why_it_matters: You can solve problems directly instead of relying on step-by-step instructions that may be misunderstood.

bullets.point: Live interaction replaces asynchronous messaging for faster and clearer communication.
bullets.why_it_matters: This reduces delays and improves the quality of support as workflows become more advanced.

bullets.point: Intercom is optimized for messaging-based support with structured conversations and automation.
bullets.why_it_matters: This works well for scalable support, but lacks deeper real-time interaction capabilities.

bullets.point: Workflows include routing, automation, and message-based engagement tools.
bullets.why_it_matters: These features support efficiency, but do not replace live interaction for complex issues.

bullets.point: Conversations are managed through chat and asynchronous communication channels.
bullets.why_it_matters: This keeps processes consistent, but introduces delays when immediate guidance is needed.

sections.heading: How each tool can break down

items.fails_when: Acquire starts to break when most support interactions are simple and do not require video or co-browsing.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Intercom if messaging-based workflows are sufficient and simpler to manage.

items.fails_when: Intercom starts to break when complex issues require real-time interaction, visual guidance, or direct communication beyond messaging.
items.what_to_do_instead: Use Acquire when support depends on live interaction through video and co-browsing.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This verdict might flip if your support workload is mostly simple conversations that can be handled through messaging without needing real-time interaction. In that case, Intercom’s messaging system may be more efficient.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Pick Acquire if you need video chat and co-browsing for support.
Pick Intercom if your support is primarily messaging-based.
If real-time interaction is required, choose Acquire.

faqs.q: Which tool better matches this priority?
faqs.a: Acquire fits this need better because Acquire built-in video chat allows agents to communicate with customers face-to-face during support sessions. Intercom fails first when support is primarily messaging-based without built-in video and co-browsing capabilities.

faqs.q: When should I choose Intercom instead?
faqs.a: Choose Intercom over Acquire when most support interactions are simple and do not require video or co-browsing. Otherwise, Acquire remains the better fit for this comparison.

faqs.q: What makes Intercom fail first here?
faqs.a: Intercom fails first here when support is primarily messaging-based without built-in video and co-browsing capabilities. That is the point where Acquire becomes the stronger pick.

faqs.q: Is this verdict only about one feature?
faqs.a: No. Acquire beats Intercom because Acquire built-in video chat allows agents to communicate with customers face-to-face during support sessions, while Intercom loses once support is primarily messaging-based without built-in video and co-browsing capabilities.
